response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend a few times monthly on social media

response_parsed.opinion: At my age, having worked as an electrician for decades here in Milan, I have learned that verification is everything. When you wire a building incorrectly, people get hurt. The same principle applies to information on social media, though I must admit I am not on these platforms very often, perhaps a few times each month at most. When I do log on, the amount of false information I encounter troubles me deeply. As someone who values precision and careful work, seeing how easily misinformation spreads feels like watching someone ignore basic safety protocols. My approach has become quite methodical, much like how I would approach a complex electrical problem. First, I have learned to pause before believing or sharing anything that seems designed to provoke strong emotions. In my experience, truth rarely needs to shout. When I see something that makes bold claims, especially about health matters or political issues, I take the time to verify it through established Italian news sources like Corriere della Sera or RAI. This careful approach comes naturally to me given my professional background where mistakes have real consequences. I also find that limiting my time on these platforms helps tremendously. Since I only check social media occasionally, I am less overwhelmed by the constant stream of content. This distance gives me perspective. My faith also guides me here, reminding me that bearing false witness harms our community. When I do encounter something suspicious, I try to gently point it out to others, though I admit this can be exhausting and sometimes feeds into my already melancholic disposition. The isolation I sometimes feel is only deepened when I see how these false narratives divide people. My advice, shaped by years of technical work requiring accuracy, is simple: treat information like you would treat electrical work. Check your sources, verify before you act, consult trusted authorities, and remember that shortcuts in pursuit of truth can be dangerous. We must be diligent stewards of what we share, just as I have always been diligent with my craft.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is a 72-year-old self-employed electrician from Milan with high conscientiousness and low extraversion, who rarely uses social media. His professional background emphasizing precision and safety protocols naturally translates to a methodical, careful approach to information verification. His depression and low social media usage suggest he would find the fake content landscape draining. His moderate openness means he is willing to engage with the topic but not overly enthusiastic. His Christian faith and Italian background inform his values about truth-telling. His age and education level suggest traditional information sources would be trusted. The analogy to electrical work leverages his occupational expertise while his limited social media time explains a more detached, observational perspective rather than deep platform engagement.
response_parsed.tone: Thoughtful, methodical, somewhat weary, drawing on professional expertise, measured and careful in expression, occasionally melancholic
